coredata.dc:title: Sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe based on chitosan carbon dots and calcein for Alkaline phosphatase detection and bioimaging in cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Mu, Xiaowei
Jiang, Xue
Zhang, Yu
Liu, Xin
Zhang, Siqi
Wang, Wei
Huang, Yibing
Ma, Pinyi
Song, Daqian
coredata.dc:description: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a commonly used marker in clinical practice, and this enzyme is a key indicator for diagnosing various diseases. In this study, we describe the development of a reliable and novel fluorescent assay for ALP detection based on chitosan carbon dots (C-CDs, peak emission, 412 nm) and calcein (peak emission, 512 nm). In the presence of Eu3+ (which binds calcein), the fluorescence intensity of calcein is quenched. Utilizing the ALP-triggered generation of phosphate ions (PO4 3−) from the substrate p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP), the Eu3+ ions bind PO4 3− (which shows a higher affinity toward Eu3+ than calcein), and the fluorescence of calcein is recovered. As a consequence, C-CDs fluorescence is decreased by inner filter effect (IFE). Exploiting these changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio of C-CDs and calcein, we developed a high sensitivity, accurate, and easily synthesized ratiometric fluorescence probe. Our novel fluorescent bioassay demonstrates good linear relationship in the 0.09–0.8 mU mL−1 range, with a low detection limit of 0.013 mU mL−1. The excellent applicability of this novel assay in HepG2 cells and human serum samples demonstrates that our novel method has excellent biomedical research and disease diagnosis prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Carbon dots
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots derived from chitosan and ethylenediamine
Detection of ALP in human serum samples
Design of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system
ALP detection in human serum samples
Cell viability of C-CDs and imaging study

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22074052 and 22004046 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of C-CDs; (B) size distribution of C-CDs; (C) FTIR spectrum of C-CDs and chitosan; (D) XPS survey spectra of C-CDs; (E) XPS survey spectra of C-CDs for C1s; (F) XPS survey spectra of C-CDs for N1s.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-CDs and calcein at different excitation wavelengths; (B) UV–vis absorbance spectra of calcein (red line), and fluorescence emission spectra of C-CDs (blue line) and calcein (yellow line) following excitation at 290 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence lifetimes of C-CDs (1) alone, (2) + calcein, (3) + calcein and Eu3+. C-CDs, 0.368 mg mL−1; calcein, 20 μM; Eu3+, 80 μM. (B) The change in the fluorescence spectra of the C-CDs-calcein system upon the addition of different reaction components. C-CDs, 0.368 mg mL−1; calcein, 20 μM; Eu3+, 80 μM; pNPP, 350 μM; Mg2+, 300 μM; ALP, 2 mU mL−1.

The fluorescence intensity ratio (F512/F412) of the C-CDs-calcein system after the addition of (A) Eu3+ (0, 20, 60, 100, 200, 280 μM); (B) pNPP (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 μM); and, (C) Mg2+ (20, 40, 80, 100, 200, 300, 400, 600, 800, 1000 μM).

(A) Emission spectra of the C-CDs-calcein system with increasing levels of ALP (from 0.09 to 2.0 mU mL−1); (B) The relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F512/F412) and the concentration of ALP. Inset: linear plot of F512/F412 versus ALP concentration (0.09–0.8 mU mL−1).
(A) Fluorescence spectra of the C-CDs-calcein system in the presence of other enzymes; (B) F512/F412 ratio in the presence of interfering substances (enzymes/biomolecules).

Kinetic plot of the inhibition efficiency of Na3VO4 in the presence of 2 mU mL−1 ALP vs. Na3VO4 concentration. C-CDs, 0.368 mg mL−1; calcein 20 μM; Eu3+, 80 μM; Mg2+, 300 μM; pNPP, 300 μM; pH 9.0, 50 mM Tris; Incubation temperature, 37 °C.

(A) HepG2 cell viability after incubation with different concentrations of C-CDs. Confocal fluorescence images of HepG2 cells. (B) Cells incubated with C-CDs and calcein; (C) Cells incubated with C-CDs, calcein, and Eu3+; (D) Cells incubated with C-CDs, calcein, Eu3+, and pNPP. (E) The ratio of the average fluorescence intensities of the green and blue channels. C-CDs, 0.05 mg mL−1; calcein, 10 μM; Eu3+, 20 μM; pNPP, 200 μM. Scale bar, 20 μM. Excitation, 405 nm; Emission, 425–485 nm (blue channel), 486–576 nm (green channel). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of our novel ratiometric fluorescent strategy based on C-CDs and calcein for ALP determination.
Detection of ALP activity in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe based on chitosan carbon dots and calcein for Alkaline phosphatase detection and bioimaging in cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun, 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun, 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a commonly used marker in clinical practice, and this enzyme is a key indicator for diagnosing various diseases. In this study, we describe the development of a reliable and novel fluorescent assay for ALP detection based on chitosan carbon dots (C-CDs, peak emission, 412 nm) and calcein (peak emission, 512 nm). In the presence of Eu3+ (which binds calcein), the fluorescence intensity of calcein is quenched. Utilizing the ALP-triggered generation of phosphate ions (PO4 3−) from the substrate p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP), the Eu3+ ions bind PO4 3− (which shows a higher affinity toward Eu3+ than calcein), and the fluorescence of calcein is recovered. As a consequence, C-CDs fluorescence is decreased by inner filter effect (IFE). Exploiting these changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio of C-CDs and calcein, we developed a high sensitivity, accurate, and easily synthesized ratiometric fluorescence probe. Our novel fluorescent bioassay demonstrates good linear relationship in the 0.09–0.8 mU mL−1 range, with a low detection limit of 0.013 mU mL−1. The excellent applicability of this novel assay in HepG2 cells and human serum samples demonstrates that our novel method has excellent biomedical research and disease diagnosis prospects.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is widely expressed in the liver, bone, kidney, bile duct, and placenta [1–4]. This non-specific phosphomonolipase catalyzes the dephosphorylation of a range of substrates, including nucleic acids and alkaloids, releasing phosphate ions (PO4 3−) and free hydroxyl groups [5,6]. Abnormal ALP levels in human serum are an important biomarker for diagnosing leukemia, diabetes, osteomalacia, lymphoma, and breast cancer [7–10]. In addition, ALP is a marker for biological or pathological processes in body fluids (saliva, urine, cerebrospinal fluid, etc.) and white blood cells [11–13]. Effective early screening of diseases can be performed by extracting biomarkers such as ALP from body fluids [14]. Through early screening of diseases, life-threatening complications may be avoided, thus improving the patient's survival rate and quality of life [15]. Therefore, the development of accurate and simple ALP monitoring methods in vivo and in vitro is essential.

To date, a range of methods for detecting ALP have been developed, including the surface-enhanced Raman scattering method [16], colorimetric assays [17–19], electrochemical methods [20], chromatographic assays [21], and fluorometric assays [22,23]. While these methods all have their advantages, they each have their own disadvantages. For example, some methods require complex synthesis/modification, some rely on expensive equipment, and some are susceptible to interference (which can cause errors in the results). To overcome these disadvantages, a new ALP detection method with higher sensitivity, strong selectivity, simplicity, and stability should be developed to detect ALP in vivo and in vitro.

The current fluorescence analysis methods for ALP detection demonstrate several clear advantages over other methods, including rapid response, simple operation, high sensitivity, and good accuracy [24–26]. In the past decade, several different fluorescent assays have been developed which utilize semiconductor quantum dots, noble metal nanoclusters, organic fluorescent probes, or carbon dots as fluorometric probes to detect ALP [27–29]. However, most of these assays measure the intensity response, and the accuracy of these fluorescence analysis methods is easily affected by the external environment, photobleaching, and substrates. Unlike the single-wavelength response probes, the ratiometric fluorescence method utilizes the fluorescence intensity ratio at two different wavelengths to determine the content of the analyte [30–35]. This method provides a signal that is unperturbed by the environment and that is independent of the intensity of the light source and the instrument's sensitivity. Hence, ratiometric fluorescence methods effectively negate background interference and amplify the signal readout, thus improve the sensitivity and accuracy of detection. To date, some ratiometric fluorescent probes for detecting ALP have been reported [36–38]. However, these methods often involve complex synthesis and modification and poor photochemical stability, which affect the further applications of probes in living cells. Therefore, there is still necessary to develop a ratiometric method with simple synthesis, good stability, and low detection limit for detecting ALP. Herein, we report the preparation of convenient and effective blue-emission chitosan-doped carbon dots (C-CDs) from chitosan and ethylenediamine via the one-pot hydrothermal method. Importantly, this preparation procedure does not require complicated synthetic operations or expensive apparatus. Calcein is a membrane permeable fluorescent dye for use in living cells as an indicator for metal ions. Rare earth elements can complex with calcein and quench their fluorescence [39]. Using C-CDs and calcein, we have constructed a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe based on two well-resolved emission peaks obtained from the same excitation wavelength.

The new ratiometric fluorescence probe constructed from C-CDs and calcein is shown in Scheme 1 . Eu3+ dramatically quenches the fluorescence intensity of calcein at 512 nm. However, PO4 3− derived from the hydrolysis of p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP) by ALP demonstrates a higher affinity for Eu3+. After Eu3+ binding to PO4 3−, the quenching of calcein fluorescence by Eu3+ is decreased, and calcein fluorescence increases proportionally. As a consequence of the increase in calcein fluorescence, C-CD fluorescence is quenched. This detection method is characterized by high selectivity, low cost, and high sensitivity. Importantly, this novel probe has potential applications in ALP inhibitor screening and for monitoring endogenous ALP in human serum and HepG2 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots derived from chitosan and ethylenediamine
ce:section.ce:para: C-CDs derived from chitosan and ethylenediamine were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization. Briefly, 0.2 g Chitosan was solubilized in 10 mL of 1% acetic acid, and stirred for 30 min until the chitosan was completely dissolved. Next, 0.5 mL of ethylenediamine was added to the solution, and the mixture was autoclaved in a 20 mL Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 9 h. After cooling to room temperature, the C-CDs obtained were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The upper supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and dialyzed for 24 h in a 1000 Da cut-off dialysis bag to remove all precursor molecules. The resulting C-CDs solution (concentration, 18.4 mg mL−1) was stored at 4 °C until subsequent use.

In a typical reaction, 100 μL of 250 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0), 21.4 μL of 7 mM MgCl2 solution, 15 μL of 10 mM pNPP, and ALP (different volumes of stock) were sequentially added to a microcentrifuge tube. The reaction solution was then incubated in the dark at 37 °C for 80 min. Next, 10 μL of 18.4 mg mL−1 C-CDs, 10 μL of 1 mM calcein, and 20 μL of 2 mM Eu3+ were added to the reaction solution. Finally, deionized water was added to a final volume of 500 μL. The reaction solution was then transferred to a cuvette and incubated at room temperature for 1 min. To evaluate ALP activity, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity F512/F412 (excitation, 290 nm) was measured.

For the inhibitor assay, 50 μL of Na3VO4 aqueous solution (at concentrations from 0 μM to 600 μM) was added into the ALP reaction system (containing 100 μL of 0.25 M Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0), 15 μL of 10 mM pNPP, 21.4 μL of 7 mM MgCl2 solution, and 50 μL of 20 mU mL−1 ALP). The reaction solution was then incubated at 37 °C in a water bath for 80 min. Next, the inhibitor-treated ALP reaction solution was mixed with 10 μL of 18.4 mg mL−1 C-CDs, 10 μL of 1 mM calcein, and 20 μL of 2 mM Eu3+. Finally, deionized water was added to a final volume of 500 μL. The fluorescence assay for ALP was then performed as described in the previous section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assay ALP levels in human serum samples, human serum was first diluted 100-fold in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.5, 50 mM). A pretreated human serum sample was also spiked to obtain the ALP standard solution (0, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 mU mL−1). The fluorescent assay procedure for the detection of ALP in human serum samples was as described above for the measurement of ALP activity.

HepG2 cells were seeded in a laser confocal-transparent dish and cultured for 24 h in high glucose Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS). First, 1 mL C-CDs (0.05 mg mL−1) was added into the cell culture medium, and the cells were incubated for a further 6 h at 37 °C. Second, the cell culture medium was removed, and the dish was washed three times with PBS buffer to remove excess C-CDs. Third, the cells were incubated with calcein (10 μM) for 25 min at 37 °C, and then excess calcein was removed with a PBS buffer wash. Next, the cells were sequentially incubated with Eu3+ (20 μM) in culture medium for 5 min, and pNPP (200 μM) in culture medium for 30 min. Prior to imaging, the cell layer was rinsed with PBS buffer solution. Cell images were then obtained using an LSM 710 confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) using green and blue emission channels (excitation, 405 nm).

The C-CDs synthesized from the chitosan and ethylenediamine were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The size and morphology of the synthesized C-CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, the C-CDs are spherical particles with excellent mono-dispersity. A statistical analysis of 53 particle dots revealed that the C-CDs had a narrow diameter range (1.5–3.1 nm) with an average particle diameter of 2.3 nm.

Next, we characterized the functional groups on the surface of chitosan and C-CDs using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1C). The results reveal broad absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 represented the –OH stretching vibration on the carbon dots. Likewise, small absorption peaks at 2992 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of the C–H bond. In addition, two peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1571 cm−1 can be attributed to –NH stretching vibrations, and a sharp peak at 1401 cm−1 can be attributed to the –OH bending vibration.

An XPS survey was also performed to determine the composition of C-CDs. According to the XPS full-scan spectrum (Fig. 1D), three main peaks at 284.6 eV, 400.5 eV, and 531 eV can be attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The total intensities of C 1s and O1s are larger than the intensity of N 1s, indicating that the synthesized C-CDs are mainly composed of C, O, and a small amount of N. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) is comprised of three peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.9 eV, and 288.2 eV, and these can be attributed to CO, C–C, and C–N in C-CDs, respectively. Moreover, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) demonstrates the existence of N–C (399.5 eV) and N–H (400.4 eV).

The optical properties of fluorescence excitation and emission in the C-CDs were also studied (Fig. S1). The fluorescence emission intensity of the emission peak of C-CDs gradually increased as the excitation wavelength was increased (from 280 nm to 400 nm). The optimal emission intensity for the C-CDs was observed at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. These results demonstrate substantial fluorescence emission from the surface of C-CDs [40].

The fluorescence behavior of the C-CDs and calcein at different pH values was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of C-CDs was essentially unresponsive to pH change. While the fluorescence intensity of calcein gradually increased over the pH 4–7 range, it remained stable over the pH 7–9 range, and then increased slightly at pH values greater than 9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 A, the C-CDs-calcein system demonstrated two emission peaks. While the positions and intensities of the emission peaks for C-CDs were influenced by the excitation wavelength, only the intensities of the emission peaks for calcein were affected. Taking into account emission intensities at the two wavelengths and the distance between the emission peaks, an excitation wavelength of 290 nm was selected as optimal in the following experiments. At an excitation wavelength of 290 nm, the emission peak of C-CDs was 412 nm and the emission peak of calcein was 512 nm.

To clarify the mechanism of calcein-induced fluorescence quenching, the absorption spectrum of calcein and the fluorescence emission spectra of C-CDs and calcein were investigated further. Calcein was revealed to have a maximum absorption peak in the 400 nm–500 nm range, and there was a large area overlap between calcein absorption and the emission peak of the carbon dots (Fig. 2B).
The ability of calcein to quench C-CDs was also investigated. As expected, C-CDs quenching gradually increased with increasing concentrations of calcein (0, 20, 40, 60, 80 μM), (Fig. S3).

The fluorescence lifetimes of C-CDs and the C-CDs-calcein system (the black and red lines in Fig. 3 A) were also investigated. The lifetime of the C-CDs and the C-CDs-calcein system was observed as 5.56 ns and 5.50 ns, respectively. Since the ultraviolet absorption band of calcein overlaps with the excitation band of C-CDs, this decay in the fluorescence intensity provides evidence that the IFE effect dominates the quenching mechanism [41]. After Eu3+ was added, the fluorescence lifetime of the C-CDs-calcein system is hardly changed (5.57 ns). This can be attributed to chelate formation between calcein and Eu3+. As shown in Fig. 3B, several additional measurements were conducted to validate the proposed system for the sensitive detection of ALP activity. When C-CDs (0.368 mg mL−1) and calcein (20 μM) were introduced into this system, two strong fluorescence peaks (412 nm and 512 nm) were observed (excitation, 290 nm). Addition of Eu3+ (80 μM) to system (C-CDs + calcein + Eu3+) effectively quenched the fluorescence signal from calcein. Further addition of ALP (2 mU mL−1) to system (C-CDs + calcein + Eu3+ + ALP) induced only a slight change in fluorescence intensity. However, when 7 mU mL−1 ALP was introduced into a mixture of C-CDs, calcein, Eu3+, MgCl2, and pNPP, the fluorescence intensity of calcein was markedly enhanced, recovering to approximately 60% of the original value. The above results demonstrate the feasability of the proposed method for the detection of ALP.

Prior to the application of this method in ALP assays, we optimized the reaction conditions (including the concentrations of Eu3+, pNPP, and Mg2+, the reaction time, and the pH) to acquire the best experimental results. After the addition of Eu3+ (0–280 μM) into the C-CDs-calcein system, the fluorescence intensity of calcein (512 nm) decreased gradually (Fig. S4). As noted above, Eu3+ can complex with calcein to quench it's fluorescence. When the concentration of Eu3+ reached 100 μM, the ratio of F512/F412 stabilized (Fig. 4 A). Therefore, 100 μM Eu3+ was selected for all subsequent experiments.

Next, the impact of pNPP concentration on the C-CDs-calcein system was evaluated. Fig. 4B reveals that the F512/F412 ratio increased with pNPP concentration up to 300 μM, after which the ratio decreased slightly. Accordingly, we chose 300 μM as the optimal concentration of pNPP.

Mg2+ ions are known to promote ALP catalysis. The F512/F412 ratio was observed to increase with increasing Mg2+ concentration (over the 0–1000 μM range). However, F512/F412 was essentially stable at Mg2+ concentrations higher than 300 μM (Fig. 4C). Therefore, 300 μM of Mg2+ was chosen as the optimal concentration.

The effect of reaction time (over the 0–100 min range) at 37 °C on the F512/F412 ratio is shown in Fig. S5. The results reveal that F512/F412 remains constant after a reaction time of 80 min. The impact of pH on the C-CDs-calcein system was also investigated (Fig. S6). The F512/F412 ratio was observed to increase with pH, reaching a maximum at pH 9. Hence, 80 min and pH 9 were selected as the optimum reaction time and pH value for this system.

Under these optimal conditions, the proficiency of the C-CDs-calcein-Eu3+ system for fluorescence determination of ALP was assessed. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity at 412 nm gradually decreased while the fluorescence intensity at 512 nm increased with the addition of different concentrations of ALP (from 0.09 to 2 mU mL−1).

A good linear correlation between fluorescence intensity ratio and increasing concentrations of ALP (range, 0.09–0.8 mU mL−1) was obtained (Fig. 5B inset). The regression equation for the linear fit was Y = 20.37X + 1.023 (R = 0.9971). The ultralow limit of detection (LOD) was estimated as 0.013 mU mL−1, and the signal-to-noise ratio was 3.

To evaluate the specificity of the C-CDs-calcein system, we investigated the effects of various enzymes (20 mU mL−1 of ACP, β-GC, HAase, HRP, and TG) on the fluorescence spectra of this system under assay conditions (Fig. 6 A). In addition, we examined the effects of these enzymes and potential interfering biomolecules (0.25 g L−1 of ATP, BSA, GSH, and Cys) on the corresponding cell imaging assay (using 2 mU mL−1 ALP) (Fig. 6B). Neither the enzymes nor the biomolecules interfered with the F512/F412 ratio (Fig. 6A and B), providing firm evidence that selectivity in the established assay is satisfactory.

Sodium orthovanadate (Na3VO4) is a known inhibitor for ALP, protein tyrosine phosphatase, and many ATPases. The VO4 3− ion of Na3VO4 forms a reversible bond with the active site of the phosphatase, thus inhibiting phosphatase-dependent dephosphorylation of substrate. Because of its potent inhibitory activities towards ALP, Na3VO4 is widely employed in the evaluation of ALP activity [23].

In enzyme reaction kinetics, the formula for inhibition efficiency is defined as:
100 Inhibition efficiency (%) = [(F512/F412)without_inhibitor–(F512/F412)inhibitor]/[(F512/F412)without_inhibitor–(F512/F412]0)] ×

From the above equation, the half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) can be defined as the concentration of inhibitor at 50% total inhibition. The lower the IC50, the stronger the inhibitory effect. The relationship between inhibition efficiency and Na3VO4 concentration is shown in Fig. 7 . The IC50 of Na3VO4 for ALP is estimated to be 1.21 μM, which is much lower than reported for other assays [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP detection in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the practicality of the ALP ratiometric fluorescent sensing system, we used human serum samples as a model for quantitative determination. The procedure is elaborated in the experimental section, and the results are summarized in Table 1 . The ALP in normal human serum is largely derived from liver and bone, showing a normal range of ∼20–120 mU mL−1 serum [43]. As shown in Table 1, after the human serum sample was diluted 100-fold, 0.295–0.297 mU mL−1 ALP can be detected in the unspiked human serum samples. The relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) for different spiked samples was less than 4.36%, demonstrating the promising potential of the C-CDs-calcein system for ALP measurements in real serum samples. Furthermore, we measured ALP levels in the above human serum samples using commercial ALP ELISA kits. An ELISA calibration curve was constructed using the ALP standard solution at concentrations ranging from 0 to 240 mU mL−1 (Fig. S6). Measure the absorbance with a microplate reader at a wavelength of 450 nm, and calculate the ALP concentration in human serum samples from the standard curve. The results were consistent with the results obtained by our method, indicating the sensing platform could reliably assay ALP in the human serum sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability of C-CDs and imaging study
ce:section.ce:para: ALP is widely distributed in the human liver, bones, kidneys, and other tissues. In general, high endogenous ALP in clinical samples indicates hepatobiliary diseases. To evaluate ALP bioimaging in living cells, we first explored the effect of different concentrations of C-CDs on the viability of HepG2 cells. The viability of HepG2 cells incubated with 0–0.09 mg mL−1 C-CD is shown in Fig. 8 A. The results reveal that HepG2 cell viability exceeds 80% at high concentrations of C-CDs (≤0.05 mg mL−1), providing evidence that the C-CDs we synthesized have high intracellular compatibility and can be used in vivo to good effect.

Given the good biocompatibility of C-CDs and the excellent selectivity of the ALP sensing system, application of the new ratiometric assay to intracellular ALP bioimaging was evaluated. The morphology of HepG2 cells after incubation with C-CDs and calcein is shown in Fig. 8B.

HepG2 cells treated with C-CDs-calcein exhibited weak blue C-CDs fluorescence and strong green calcein fluorescence. This result demonstrates that the two components easily penetrate the cell membrane and/or are transferred to the cell through endocytosis. Moreover, this result provides evidence for IFE between C-CDs and calcein. After treatment with Eu3+, the fluorescence intensity of the green channel decreased due to static quenching of calcein by Eu3+ (Fig. 8C). When pNPP was added to the medium (30 min incubation), the pNPP was hydrolyzed by endogenous ALP to generate PO4 3−. Because the PO4 3− product is able to combine with Eu3+, free calcein was released. As a consequence of calcein release, calcein fluorescence recovers and C-CDs fluorescence is quenched. As shown in Fig. 8D, the fluorescence intensity of calcein (green channel) in HepG2 cells increased, and the fluorescence intensity of C-CDs (blue channel) was quenched.

Fig. 8E depicts the ratio of the average fluorescence intensity of the green and blue channels. The FGreen/FBlue ratios for the three sets of images (8B, 8C, 8D) are 1.93, 1.48, and 3.19, respectively. These results provide firm evidence that our novel ratiometric fluorescent probe is suitable for the biological imaging of ALP in living cells.

The important characteristics of several recently reported fluorescence sensing methods used for ALP detection (including synthetic materials, sensing mode, linear range, LOD, and applications) are listed in Table S1. The comparative advantages of our novel method include high sensitivity, ultralow LOD, and a simple synthesis method. Most importantly, compared with single fluorescent off-on methods, results obtained with our ratiometric probes are independent of light source and instrument sensitivity. In addition to the above advantages, our novel method may be used in inhibitor screening, the evaluation of actual samples, and the bioimaging of endogenous ALP.

We successfully prepared C-CDs from chitosan and ethylenediamine using a convenient, and low-cost one-step hydrothermal method. Considering the importance of precise ALP detection in clinical diagnostics, a ratiometric fluorescence method was developed based on these C-CDs and calcein. This method provided good linearity with an LOD of 0.013 mU mL−1 for ALP. The method was also successfully validated using human serum samples, and excellent spiked recoveries were achieved. The C-CDs-calcein system exhibited good biocompatibility toward HepG2 cells. Thus, our novel method may be used in the intracellular monitoring and imaging of endogenous ALP. The novel ratiometric fluorescence method detailed in this study may offer an effective tool for simple detection of ALP with high sensitivity and good selectivity.

Xiaowei Mu: Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Xue Jiang: Data curation, Software. Yu Zhang: Data curation, Software. Xin Liu: Methodology. Siqi Zhang: Validation. Wei Wang: Methodology. Yibing Huang: Funding acquisition, Resources. Pinyi Ma: Supervision, Project administration, Writing-Reviewing. Daqian Song: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22074052 and 22004046).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.339163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanistic and evolutionary insights from comparative enzymology of phosphomonoesterases and phosphodiesterases across the alkaline phosphatase superfamily
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Sunden, I. AlSadhan, A.Y. Lyubimov, S. Ressl, H. Wiersma-Koch, J. Borland, C.L. Brown, Jr., T.A. Johnson, Z. Singh, D.
